summary: This short 2005 post is a reader-contributed correction about lithium-ion battery technology for power tools. Kevin, who built an electric VW Rabbit, corrects Dan's earlier claims about battery chemistry in Milwaukee tools. The discussion covers Li-Ion discharge rates, efficiency, and cost barriers. The post highlights that while Li-Ion batteries offer impressive power-to-weight ratios, their high cost ($145 for a single battery) remained the key obstacle to adoption in consumer power tools at the time.
key_points: Li-Ion batteries discharge at rates measured in 'C', with top-of-line cells reaching 10-15C while maintaining 80% efficiency
A 22.2V Li-Ion battery can deliver 555 watts (nearly 1 HP) while weighing just over half a pound
The primary barrier to Li-Ion adoption in power tools was cost, not performance
Most power tools at the time used Ni-MH or Ni-Cad, not Li-Ion
money_quotes: Since he's built an electric rabbit (VW, not bunny), I'm inclined to believe him.
A 1C discharge means the battery is empty in 1 hour. At 2C, empty in 30 min.
That's 555 Watts or almost 1 HP. That's one serious battery operated tool.
And the battery only weights a bit over 1/2 a pound. The problem is the battery costs $145.
key_insight: In 2005, lithium-ion battery technology already had the power and weight advantages to revolutionize cordless tools, but cost was the remaining barrier to widespread adoption.
